slides.title: Community and Environment
bullets: Prepare to read, discuss and write
slides.narration: Welcome to Grade 8 English, Unit 3: Community and Environment. Welcome to Unit 3: Community and Environment. In this unit, you will explore how young people can make a difference in their communities by protecting the environment. You will read a story called "Rising Tide" about a girl named Sophia who leads a youth movement to clean up a polluted bay in a coastal Kenyan city. Through this story and the activities in this lesson, you will build your vocabulary, sharpen your grammar skills, and develop your ability to speak and write persuasively about issues that matter to you. In this short lecture, preview the key ideas and prepare for the independent lesson.

slides.title: What you will achieve
bullets: Read and understand a complex narrative about community activism and environmental protection
Use 33 new vocabulary words accurately in speaking and writing
Construct clauses that end with prepositions naturally and correctly
slides.narration: By the end of this unit, you will be able to read and understand a complex narrative about community activism and environmental protection. you will also use 33 new vocabulary words accurately in speaking and writing. you will also construct clauses that end with prepositions naturally and correctly. Keep these goals in mind as you work.

slides.title: Language for precise thinking
slides.narration: Your vocabulary work begins with these words: stifle, wane, commemorate, articulate, hasten, advocate. Listen to each pronunciation in the vocabulary lab, study its meaning in context, and use the word accurately in your own discussion and writing.

slides.title: Read critically
slides.narration: The reading sequence includes Community and Environment: source text 1 and Community and Environment: source text 2. Read actively. Identify each text's central ideas, notice viewpoint and reliability, and support every inference with relevant evidence from the text.

slides.title: Clauses Ending with Prepositions
bullets: What it means and why it matters A clause is a group of words that contains a subject and a verb. A preposition is a small word like in, on, at, from,...
slides.narration: The first language focus is Clauses Ending with Prepositions. What it means and why it matters A clause is a group of words that contains a subject and a verb. A preposition is a small word like in, on, at, from, about, to, or with that shows a relationship between words. In English, it is perfectly natural and correct to end a clause with a preposition. Understanding this pattern will help you speak and write more fluently. Notice the structure in the model examples, then apply it deliberately in connected sentences and paragraphs.

slides.title: Use English for a real purpose
bullets: Explain, support and improve your ideas
slides.narration: In speaking, you will complete Speaking 1: Discussing the story. Organise your ideas, use evidence and respond clearly to questions. In writing, you will plan, draft, check and revise. Use the success criteria before submitting your work.

slides.title: You are ready to begin
slides.narration: You are ready to begin. Start with the vocabulary lab, continue through reading and comprehension, apply the grammar focus, and complete the speaking and writing practices. Use AI English for hints and feedback, and bring your questions to the live sessions.
